speaker: interviewer
text: --tape 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I survived till now, I'll be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kaufman, what day were you liberated on?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it? January-- no, May the 10th--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what liberation-- what-- how did you first hear that you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We see-- you could-- we could see that first of all, that the German ran out. Schindler-- two of my friends, really close-- very close friends, who came to Vancouver even to visit me, they took him in his car because they were looking for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Oskar Schindler tell you that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he took us together and made, like, a meeting and tell us, you're free. You're liberated. You can go wherever. You're free to go wherever you want to. And he, he and his wife and two or three Jewish boys took him in the car. And they left with him. And the German ran away. So we were free. We didn't know what to do with that freedom. Nobody is surviving us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the spirit at the factory the day of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian came right away in. The Russian came in. And they made another-- took us together. And as long as I will live, I will remember the speech what they made there. That was the NKVD. He said, you're liberated. You went through a lot. But now, you're free to go any place you wanna. But one thing you will remember, that we, the Russian, liberate. That was really a political thing to do it. And he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were free. And everybody tried to worry. Like myself, I went-- there was a little lake. And I went by myself. [SOBBING] And I sat and cried. What I'm going to do? [SOBBING] First in my mind was to go to Cracow to see if my niece is alive. Which niece?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my brother's daughter, who the-- the Jewish people were keeping her.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she put into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940-- right away from the ghetto. They want children-- no children in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you where she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my sister-in-law because she give them so much money, whatever she had, jewelry or whatever, to keep the baby. They were poor people. And that was OK. So my mind was-- I knew my sister-in-law was not alive. My brother, I didn't know. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to-- I remember like now on the lake. And I was sitting and crying. So I said, I'm hungry. Nobody gives me nothing. You know, We were missing that piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back. And there the Russian made a meeting from all of us, took us together. And now I am-- and I remember what he said. Wherever your destiny is going to take you, wherever it's going to happen to you, one thing you will never forget as long as you live, that we are the one who brought you the freedom. And they were right. You see, that was really, again, [LAUGHS] a political speech. Because-- and from there I had already a few guys, young fellows like me. And I said, I have to-- one place I have to go is to Cracow. I have to see if my niece is alive. And they stole a horse and a buggy. And we took a bed from the camp and put on the top of that. And we started to go close-- every little town, we stopped. We were hungry. First time after five years we were hungry to go ourself to care about-- to get the food. Because before, everything was given to us-- leaving-- the piece of bread was given to us. So we went down. I was the one nominated to go down near the city to bring the-- so the Czech-- we went to Czechoslovakia, to-- because that was in Czechoslovakia-- and to, to Poland. And wherever we stopped-- because we were still in the uniforms. Nobody-- everybody could recognize us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- the bakeries, they said, please, wait another hour or something. I'm putting a cake in the oven. And you will have it. So bread and cake, they give it to us. So we were asking, begging for that until we hit the Polish border. Polish border, we came. And I never forget what they said. They said, Jesus, Maria, so many-- so many Jews arriving. We were five-- first ones, OK? So you see, they didn't like the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went there. And the only one who knew my niece is, is my friend who lives in Montreal. Because I said, if I don't survive, there's a little girl over there. You take out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came for my little niece, she said, somebody is looking for you. And only person who knew-- because I was scared-- was my friend. I knew she was there already. So we met. And we were [INAUDIBLE]. Can you describe the-- what the reunion was like between you and your niece?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a little girl. She was, at that time, maybe seven years old. I don't know if she was five because she was a little thing. And I took her. I had a-- some people give me an apartment. And she slept with me. And the one bed was for this guy. The other one was-- you know, we shared together. And I had that apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every night she was crying, crying, and crying. And I said, what do you want? You want to go back? I take you back. You want something-- to buy you something? I buy you something. I didn't know what she wanted. And she said she wants a Jesus.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- I went out, and I bought a picture of Jesus. And I hanged that on my bed. And every night, the little girl was kneeling down and saying her prayers. And as soon as she finished prayers, she stopped crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's my story about the liberation with my little girl, who is-- lives now in Lugano, Switzerland, very religious, very religious. My grandson visited her. So I told him, don't go Friday night, please, because they won't open the door-- the Shabbos, OK? So he went after, waited [LAUGHS] on Saturday when we finished to go. But thank God she's alive. I love her, and she loves me. Did you have an opportunity to go back to your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I went-- as a matter of fact, I told you the story that we hide some merchandise. And after the war, I took the police with me because there was a hairdresser there. And the business was three times the-- big than the apartment here, was three, four chairs. But they got for free. So-- and I went to have my hair done with my girlfriend who lives in Montreal. I said, [? Hanka, ?] I have to have my hair done. And I recognized that nothing was touched there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took the police. And I took out some merchandise with my friends. But I was really hiding with, with some painter because I needed to paint-- to paint overnight the ceiling, so the German woman wouldn't recognize in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told her I have to sleep there because they're breaking into stores. So I can sacrifice and sleep there. And I slept there. But I didn't sleep. We just packed in the whole thing-- sad story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was very romantic. It was after 12 o'clock. He was in Brunnlitz. We were getting a bowl of soup. And he came over with me. I introduced myself. And I said, I know you. You know me, too? He was very correct, very gentleman. And he was the one who stole the horse. This one stole another-- I came with four boyfriends home. So one day, I had to [LAUGHS] make up my mind. And we got married in Cracow-- to have 10 people. I went to a rabbi, got married. And those 10 people, I had to go on the street and ask a Jew who was passing. I said, please, come in because I need some help. It has to be 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came, and then we made the chuppah. And that's it. And I was happily married for 40-something years, 49, I think. How soon after liberation did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you decide between the four boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very easy. I tell them all to go. I swear to God, I told them all to go. I said, I-- you know, because they were all sitting at a table. Nobody said a word.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, to me, I didn't survive a war just to be bought now. And this one was persisting, was [LAUGHS] coming back. And that's it. He was very good. How did you discover that your two brothers had survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? They came to Cracow. And one moved to Antwerp. And he had a heart attack and died in Belgium. And one is-- lives in Calgary, what I just came back from there, the youngest one, yeah. He had a stroke already, and I think a heart attack. But he's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you married your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: You ask me what happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to get some papers to leave. So it was about a year or maybe two. I don't remember really what. We came-- I should remember because '54, we came to Vancouver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you between Poland and Vancouver?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, really. We went to Czechoslovakia because we had to cross the border. We couldn't get the passports from there, you know. It was-- we were just fighting to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Vancouver, how did the community greet you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it was very easy because I had the whole family here. My grandparents were dead already. But my mother's sister was there. My mother's brother-- two brothers were still living here and cousins. And I still have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they want to know about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. It was too sad to hear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever try to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but you cannot talk when no one is listening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your husband talk about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were together all the time. I didn't have to tell him nothing. He didn't have to tell me nothing. He was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband do when he moved to Vancouver?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he moved to Vancouver, he was working in my uncle's factory. He was a cutter. How he became a cutter, I don't know. But he knew better the cutting business. But he never-- just finished high school, that's it. And from there, he wanted to go-- he took a course in English. He spoke better English than I do. And he got from the night school the best student from all [LAUGHS] Vancouver. And after he went, took a real estate course. And there, he got from the [LAUGHS] real estate board the best student from real estating. And he did all right in real estate. He was really a hustler, very hard worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have children? Yes, I have one daughter, who you will find may be in the picture with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lyliane--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and two grandchildren, Ricki and Todd.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you enjoy doing today, Mrs. Kaufman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since my husband died, I don't enjoy nothing. My life is completely different because we were traveling around. We were never home. And, you know, he was lucky. He was successful in real estate. And right now is-- I live in a very big house. And I had to sell the house. I never went back since he died. Because-- and he just came home from lunch and dropped on, on the floor. And that was it, a second, didn't have time to talk. He had a heart attack. And that's it-- one second. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right now is a quiet life. I break hearts a lot. And that's it. Mrs. Kaufman, have you ever had the opportunity to meet Oskar Schindler--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went to Yad Vashem-- to Israel. And I went to Yad Vashem. And my husband cries in one corner, and I cry in the other corner because we had-- we lost everybody. So he comes, my husband, to me, he said, that's enough for you. Go out. And I go out, like, on a little balcony from there. And there's a little desk. And I seen the gentleman sitting there, writing. And I looked at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was-- a guy was very skinny, with a beautiful-- he always had-- surrounded by woman. And a gorgeous woman stay beside him. And, and I said to my husband, you know, that's Schindler? And he said, you see, I told you. Don't cry so much because you imagine already things. Go out. And I went to the woman. I said, could you tell me what's the man's name what's sitting there writing? And she said, that's Oskar Schindler. So I went to him. And we made movies-- we're together because my husband had the camera with him. But I had the movies. But I don't where the camera with the movie because I never packed-- when I moved out from the house, I, I couldn't do nothing. So Lyliane took over. And she-- but whatever happened, I don't know. And that was with us I've seen him after the war. But he was so changed. He was so skinny already. But he was an alcoholic. A good man-- he saved lots of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only I can say what he saved us, was 1,200. Like, one day, they brought-- if you have a minute, I can still talk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Came a transport from someplace from Europe. And that was wintertime. And everything was frozen. We could hear only some voices inside. He opened that transport. And people start to fall out because they were dead, frozen to death. But there was some life-- were the few who had life. We asked them to go out. They didn't want to because they were scared that's Auschwitz, or whatever it is, especially they had to go under the shower, the warm water, to defrost completely. They were scared to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the boys were going. The women were going, just to show them that it's life. You can stay here. You, you are free. You were with Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was during the war that transport came over-- beautiful guys, beautiful, young fellows. And they were coming-- when they opened the doors, it was just like sardines, you know, falling out-- that frozen. Because in Poland, the winters-- you know, in Auschwitz, when they were-- make Appell and the snow was coming to me, you know, it was reminding me the tree was shaking, back and forth, because we were holding each other to be warm. And the same dress-- I was going on that wooden bed-- with the bed with the bench-- bunks what we had. [SIGHS] And, and the steam was coming out. And by the time the steam stopped a little bit, we had to go out because they were counting us again at night, counting without stopping. Because we were not working. Auschwitz, we never worked one day. We were three and a half weeks, I think, in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, we were lucky we survived, very lucky, especially Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like my girlfriend was standing next to me. And she said, Esther, what smells like that? And we were naked. Just going in, and Mengele with the stick-- Mengele, he was in charge of Auschwitz-- with a stick, 1, 2, 3. And she looks at me. And she said, ah, I think woman with big breasts going on the-- there. She said, I think if you have small breasts, you're no good. Do you smell something? she said to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, we're going in. So that's it. Well, let's go together. We were-- we were hundred percent sure we're going into the fire. But we were lucky. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because then they came, the-- some news from Schindler not to kill us, not to do nothing. He, he needs us. And he really didn't need us. He was-- just wanted to save us. That's it-- unbelievable. I would never see-- I will never forget him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what you said to him at Yad Vashem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- you know, he, he looked different. I looked different already because that was quite a few years after. We kiss each other. We made pictures. We said goodbye. That's all. Thanked-- that's all. What could I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first time that you spoke about your experiences during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it took me quite a few years. The first time what I really said the story was after the Schindler-- my husband made tapes, because he lived through the same thing what I did, with that university in Cracow. In Cracow-- I said in Vancouver in my-- the tape. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But I couldn't do it. I'm surprised how I'm making now. And-- but when Schindler's movie came out, we went to see it. I watched the movie, and I didn't see nothing, OK? I was just crying, and that's it. But I seen young people sitting and crying. And I was thinking to myself, can I keep the story in me about that guy who saved us in the pictures there? I said, that's it. I'm starting to talk. And I didn't shut up till now. It's true. Do you have any last messages that you would leave-- like to leave to your grandchildren, that you would like to leave to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just be good to other people. People are good all over the world. And hopefully, we will never have to go through that [SOBBING] and they don't have to go through that what I went. And what I am alive, I was just lucky. [CRYING] That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kaufman--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't smarter than anybody else. And I wasn't mean with some-- to somebody, what some saved their life this way. I would never do it because life is too precious. But if you can help somebody, help. Do. Well, hopefully, we don't have to go through that. and the war, either myself or my children, grandchildren, especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one last question for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you ever go to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not me-- too bad memories. I have no strength anymore to do that. I'm not a youngster. And I can't do it. Lots of people went, but I can't do it. I will go any place where you want me to go, but not to Poland. Every stone would be memories for me. And I can't do it. I cannot live anymore. [SOBBING] I take two sleeping pills to sleep, 10 pills a day. I can't do it. I can't. My life is [SOBBING] not the end. But I had a good life with my husband. So that's-- a very good life, 40-something years, unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So [SIGHS] I can't do it. To Poland, I wouldn't go. Still in the country, I went to-- we had to go to Germany to go home to England. We were in Europe traveling. So the only country what I got drunk-- I shouldn't say that [LAUGHS] on the tape, a woman drunk. [LAUGHS] Geez, my husband will kill me. I, I had one drink. And I, I said to my husband, give me another one. When I hear that German language, I tell you, I couldn't take it. So I was just drinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And suddenly, I said to my husband, you know, you better take me to the room. He said, what do you mean, take you to the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where I am. So he took me in. By tomorrow, we left. It's another country what I wouldn't go. But I would-- I wouldn't mind to go to the museum in Washington. And hopefully, I will. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's it. I'm not a healthy woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kaufman, I appreciate how painful this was for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I appreciate you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very, very much from the bottom of my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. I want to thank you, everybody of you. Really, that was really lovely. It was just-- you can-- you can't do nothing. said I said whatever. Lots of things I don't remember. I'm sorry I had to say I don't remember because I really didn't remember. I don't remember anymore. And, and maybe I push away something from me. Lots of things I pushed away because you can't live with memory. I had the-- a daughter to raise. And I wanted her to be normal like any child. And she had a normal life. I didn't talk-- I didn't-- now, they're getting to a point that she has to know about those things. And the kids-- the little ones know. Because it's good to know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she is very helpful. It's too bad that my husband is not alive. That's all. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, thank you again, Mrs. Kaufman.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you all and especially all your viewer. Thank you very much. I really appreciate--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because you're doing wonderful job. I wish-- I wish I can do something. I tried my best.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just did.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not, not enough. That's not enough for me, really. I very appreciate. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
